text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic myomectomy on a large posterior fibroid measuring approximately eight by eight centimeters. After stabilizing the uterus, an incision is made along the most prominent aspect of the fibroid and its capsule. A Bissinger bipolar device is used for dissection to maintain a bloodless surgical field.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to completely enucleate the fibroid, depositing it into the right iliac fossa. Laparoscopic suturing begins with a quill suture, performed in a multi-layered fashion to obliterate the cavity depth and overlap the capsule walls, minimizing exposure to the peritoneum.

The surgeon demonstrates the baseball suture technique, arranging the myometrium layers to minimize intramyometrial hematoma formation and enhance tissue strength. Three laparoscopic ports are used: two lateral and one central at the suprapubic region, which is later used for morcellation to remove the fibroid from the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon uses three needle holders to perform laparoscopic suturing over the myoma bed. One needle holder stabilizes the edge of the myoma and maintains uterine position, while the other two maneuver the needle through the myoma bed. The suturing is performed gently and quickly to minimize blood loss before the vasopressin effect diminishes. Barbed sutures are used, allowing suturing without knotting, which saves time. The procedure concludes with a bloodless plane of dissection.
